key_insight: Forgetting in continual learning is framed as task interference: when new learning overlaps and conflicts with old tasks, the lost performance equals the interference energy, and can be reduced only when task structure allows separation or orthogonal sharing.
engineering_relevance: This provides a mechanistic basis for designing replay-free continual-learning systems that preserve useful prior knowledge while allocating new capacity only where tasks truly conflict.
method: Models forgetting as interference rather than a post-hoc correction.
Uses path-averaged curvature in deep networks to estimate interference with minimal extra forward passes.
Introduces Interference-Gated Functional Allocation (IGFA), which shares aligned directions and protects conflicting ones.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that IGFA achieves lossless retention on structurally separable tasks and matches or exceeds strong replay-free structural baselines on dissimilar-task streams.
watch_item: The approach depends on estimating task similarity/interference accurately, so performance may degrade when curvature or support structure is noisy or hard to measure in practice.
paper_title: Interference and Retention in Continual Learning
